text.heading: Truth emerging on PTDF looting
text.p: SIR: Recently, in the course of the appropriation bill defences and exercises at the National Assembly, the Executive Secretary of Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF), Alhaji Kabir Mohammed, alleged that former President Olusegun Obasanjo misapplied the PTDF resources.

In his words before the lawmakers, the Executive Secretary said "We don’t collect the Signature Bonus directly. It is usually collected by the DPR which sells the (oil) blocs and PTDF does not control that account. The PTDF does not even know what goes into or comes out of that account". According to Alhaji Kabir Mohammed, President Obasanjo as Petroleum Minister then was in charge of petroleum funds.

Nigerians and the international community would recall to a great extent the over-heated nature of our polity while President Obasanjo and Vice-President Atiku Abubakar were in the last lap of their second tenure in office in 2007. The matter bordered on the misuse and abuse of the resources of PTDF. Obasanjo and Abubakar took turns accusing each other and stating their individual cases. It was a tough period for the nation and her people. The business of government came to a standstill. The country and her people suffered. The courts were besieged and some individuals were victimised.

The Senate was disturbed and partitioned along Atiku supporters and Obasanjo’s foot soldiers. The Presidency was divided and service to the Nigerian people was sacrificed on the alter of political expediency and blackmail. The Nigerian people will never forget in haste the flagrant abuse of due process that became the order of the day in Obasanjo’s last days in office. So now that both Obasanjo and Atiku are out of office; it is easier and better for us to know the truth of what happened to the PTDF money. Objectivity will be possible now beyond the rhetoric and lies of Maina Waziri and the chicanery of Victor Ndoma-Egba and his Ad-hoc committee in the then Senate. Let there be further investigations into the matter so that the looting that took place in the Nigeria’s oil sector can be properly exposed.
Dept of Political Science, University of Jos.
